entry.title: About
entry.summary: Welcome to my portfolio! I'm Winston, a third-year student in Mathematics Education!
entry.content:
Wilbur Watts M.Ed. Candidate, Mathematics Education · Penn State University

Welcome to my portfolio. I'm Wilbur Watts — a Penn State master's student dedicated to making rigorous math education accessible to every learner, whether they're in a college classroom or a workforce training program. This space reflects my journey as both a practitioner and a scholar: the curricula I've designed, the pedagogical questions I'm exploring, and my ongoing commitment to meeting students exactly where they are.

Personal Statement

My path into mathematics education has never followed a single straight line — and I think that's made me a better teacher. Working across college mathematics and workforce programs has taught me that the students who show up in these spaces carry incredible range: different goals, different histories with math, and different definitions of what success looks like. My job, as I see it, is to design learning experiences that honor that range rather than flatten it.

Equity in mathematics education isn't an abstract principle to me — it's a design problem. It shows up in the questions I choose for a lesson, the way I sequence a concept, the assumptions I examine when a student struggles. Too often, mathematics instruction is built around the student who already arrived prepared, leaving everyone else to catch up on their own. In my work with college students and adult learners in workforce programs, I've seen the cost of that design — and I've committed myself to building something better.

Pursuing my master's degree in mathematics education at Penn State has given me the theoretical grounding to match my practical instincts. I'm especially interested in how curriculum design can serve as a tool for equity: how intentional sequencing, authentic contexts, and transparent learning goals can shift a student's relationship with mathematics from one of anxiety to one of agency. Good curriculum doesn't just deliver content — it sends a message about who belongs in the room.

Social Media

entry.title: Specialization
entry.summary: Area of SpecializationBest Example of Work
entry.content:
Area of Specialization

As a Mathematics Education student at Pennsylvania State University, my area of specialization focuses on preparing to teach secondary mathematics (grades 7–12) through equitable, research-based instructional practices. My coursework and field experiences emphasize deep conceptual understanding in algebra, geometry, calculus, and statistics, alongside the pedagogical content knowledge necessary to make these subjects meaningful and accessible to diverse learners.

I am particularly interested in fostering mathematical reasoning, problem-solving, and productive struggle in the classroom. Drawing on current research in mathematics education, I design lessons that promote inquiry, discourse, and real-world connections. I strive to create inclusive learning environments where all students see themselves as capable mathematicians and develop confidence in their quantitative and analytical skills.

Through clinical experiences in local school districts and collaboration with faculty in the College of Education, I continue to refine my instructional strategies, assessment design, and classroom management practices. My goal is to empower students not only to master mathematical content, but to think critically, communicate clearly, and apply mathematics beyond the classroom.

Best Example of Work

Deep thoughts..

entry.title: Competency
entry.content:
My professional expertise spans three interconnected domains: equity-centered mathematics curriculum design, post-secondary and workforce mathematics instruction, and the study of mathematical identity development among non-traditional learners. These areas reflect both the practical knowledge I have built through years of direct instruction and tutoring, and the scholarly grounding I have developed through graduate study at Penn State University.

Curriculum Vitae

Areas of Expertise

  • Equity-Centered Curriculum Design: I design instructional sequences, assessments, and learning materials that center student belonging and access alongside mathematical rigor, with particular focus on developmental and gateway mathematics contexts.
  • Post-Secondary & Workforce Mathematics Instruction: I teach and tutor college mathematics and adult workforce learners across a range of courses from developmental arithmetic through calculus, with expertise in differentiated instruction and formative assessment.
  • Mathematical Identity & Engagement: I investigate how students — particularly adult learners and those in workforce programs — construct or reconstruct their identities as mathematical thinkers, and how instructional environments mediate that process.
  • Pedagogical Content Knowledge: I bring deep command of undergraduate mathematics content (algebra, pre-calculus, statistics, calculus) and the research-informed pedagogical strategies most effective for those domains.
  • Supplemental Instruction & Peer Learning: I design and facilitate peer-assisted learning structures that improve outcomes in high-enrollment gateway courses.

Prior Experiences and Accomplishments

Certifications

  • CITI Program Certificate in Human Subjects Research
  • Pennsylvania Instructional I Teaching Certificate
  • Google Workspace for Education Certified Educator, Level 1
  • Supplemental Instruction Supervisor Certification
  • CRLA College Reading and Learning Association Tutor Certification, Level II

Academic Skills

  • Technical
    • Undergraduate mathematics: multivariable calculus, linear algebra, and probability
    • Applied statistics: descriptive and inferential methods, regression analysis
    • Desmos Classroom: Interactive graphing and activity design for formative assessment
    • Canvas LMS: Course architecture, assignment scaffolding, analytics
    • Microsoft Office Suite: Document production, data organization
    • Qualtrics: Survey design and data collection for program evaluation studies
    • IRB protocol preparation and human subjects research compliance (CITI certified)
  • Methodologies
    • Narrative Inquiry: I am trained in narrative analysis methods, including the collection of in-depth interviews, storied data, and narrative coding.
    • Design-Based Research (DBR): My curriculum development work in workforce programs is informed by DBR principles — iterative cycles of design, implementation, analysis, and redesign grounded in both theoretical frameworks and practical constraints.

Future Professional Aspirations

My professional aspirations are shaped by a clear sense of the kind of work I want to do and the kind of scholar-practitioner I am becoming. In the near term, my goal is to complete my master's thesis and contribute original, peer-reviewed scholarship to the mathematics education literature — specifically in the areas of adult mathematical identity and equity-centered curriculum design.

Looking further forward, I aspire to doctoral study in mathematics education, with the intention of pursuing a faculty position that allows me to sustain an active research agenda while remaining connected to teacher preparation and practitioner development. I am especially interested in programs that bridge university-based research and community college or workforce education contexts — environments that have historically been underrepresented in the scholarly literature despite serving a large and diverse student population.

I also aspire to contribute to the design of large-scale professional development programs for mathematics instructors in post-secondary and workforce settings — translating research on identity, equity, and curriculum into practical tools that working educators can actually use.

Self-Identified Boundaries

Quantitative & Mixed-Methods Research

My methodological training has been predominantly qualitative. While I have completed graduate coursework in regression analysis and contributed to program evaluation work involving quantitative outcome data, I do not yet have the depth of expertise required to independently design or lead a mixed-methods or quantitative study. I recognize this as a meaningful limitation, particularly given that funding agencies and many peer-reviewed venues expect researchers to command multiple methodological traditions.

Knowledge of K–12 Classroom Contexts

My practical experience is concentrated in post-secondary and adult learning settings. I have limited direct experience with K–12 classrooms, and my knowledge of elementary and middle school mathematics pedagogy — while informed by my graduate coursework — has not been tested through practice. This represents a gap relative to many mathematics education researchers, who draw on deep K–12 experience to inform their scholarship.
